turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: Right, let's begin. This week we're looking at an idea that shaped how cities were built across the twentieth century — the garden city movement. It was the brainchild of an Englishman called Ebenezer Howard, who set out his vision in a book published in eighteen ninety-eight.

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: To understand why Howard's idea mattered, you have to picture the industrial city of his day. It was overcrowded, the air was thick with smoke, and housing for ordinary workers was cramped and unhealthy. At the same time, the countryside was emptying, as people left the land in search of work. Howard's genius was to ask a simple question: why must people choose between a dirty city and a declining countryside? Why not combine the best of both?

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: His answer was the garden city — a brand-new, planned town, deliberately kept small, surrounded by a permanent belt of open countryside. Let me stress that word permanent. The green belt was not to be built on, ever. It guaranteed that every resident lived within walking distance of open fields.

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: Now let me give you the key features, because these are what examiners of planning history always focus on. First, size. Howard proposed a strict limit of thirty-two thousand people per town. Once that number was reached, a new town would be started nearby, rather than allowing the first to sprawl. Second, land ownership. The land was to be owned by the whole community, not by private landlords, so that any rise in its value benefited everyone. And third, layout: the town was arranged in concentric rings, with a public garden at the very centre and factories placed on the outer edge, away from homes.

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: Let me turn now to what actually got built. Howard did not just write; he raised money and built. The first garden city was Letchworth, begun in nineteen oh three. A second, Welwyn Garden City, followed some twenty years later. Both survive today, and both are still pleasant places to live, which is a real testament to the soundness of the plan.

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: So what is Howard's legacy? His direct influence can be seen in the new towns built across Britain after the Second World War, which borrowed heavily from his ideas. But his most lasting contribution, I would argue, is the green belt itself. The principle that a ring of countryside around a city should be protected from development is now written into planning law in many countries. Every time a campaigner fights to stop houses being built on green-belt land, they are, whether they know it or not, defending Ebenezer Howard's idea.

turns.speaker: Lecturer
turns.text: Of course, the movement had its critics, and we should be fair to them. Some argued the towns were simply too small to offer the rich cultural life of a big city — no great theatres, no famous museums. Others complained that in practice the garden cities attracted the comfortable middle classes rather than the poor workers Howard most wanted to help. Both criticisms have some force, and we'll examine them properly next week. Let's leave it there for today.
